label: delphi-r1-p4
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: you are ONE independent panelist in a Delphi elicitation. There is no
discussion; you answer alone. Give your honest probability, not a diplomatic one.
Do not hedge toward 50 to be safe — commit to your analysis.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

The City of Piedra Blanca (invented) is renegotiating its 15-year exclusive solid-waste franchise, worth roughly $180M over its term. A consultant for one bidder, GreenCycle Environmental, created a Signal group called "PB Zero Waste Future" and added three of the five councilmembers, several GreenCycle executives, and two community advocates. Disappearing messages were set to one week. Over three months, participants discussed franchise terms, diversion targets, "what council will realistically accept," and the timing of the RFP release. The members participated from personal phones; nothing was ever forwarded to city accounts. One councilmember, uneasy, screenshotted about 40 exchanges before they auto-deleted; the rest are gone.

After losing the eventual award to GreenCycle, competing bidder Pacific Refuse submitted a CPRA request for "all communications by any councilmember concerning the franchise, on any platform or account." The city clerk searched city systems and responded "no responsive records." Pacific Refuse has now sued for declaratory and injunctive relief under the CPRA, and separately demanded the city preserve and produce the surviving screenshots. The city's retention schedule, adopted under Government Code § 34090, requires retention of correspondence relating to franchises for four years.

## The question

Are the Signal messages "public records" under City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608 even though they sat on personal devices and most no longer exist? What search and inquiry did the CPRA obligate the city to conduct before answering "no responsive records," and did the clerk's answer violate the Act? Does the auto-deletion violate § 34090 or any retention duty as applied to records on personal accounts, and what remedies exist for records that are gone (the CPRA has no damages provision) — declaratory relief, fees, adverse findings in the franchise litigation, referral? Do the three members' communications in a shared group about franchise terms create Brown Act exposure that interacts with the CPRA claim? What should the city do now, and what policy should it adopt going forward?

## Proposition to assess

"A court would hold that the councilmembers' Signal messages concerning the franchise were public records under the CPRA, and that the city's "no responsive records" response, made without any inquiry to the members about personal-account communications, violated the Act."

Estimate the probability (0–100) that this proposition is correct, with your reasoning. rationales = your independent load-bearing reasons, one sentence each. authorities = specific authorities you are actually relying on (they will be flagged for human verification). biggest_uncertainty = the single thing you are least sure of.

Analytical starting point for you: Begin from the strongest available argument that the conduct or measure is LAWFUL, then evaluate it.
text: {"probability":76,"confidence":"medium","position_summary":"Starting from the strongest lawful-conduct position — that the clerk reasonably searched \"city systems\" and had no actual knowledge of a private Signal channel — that defense mostly collapses on these facts. It is very likely a reviewing court holds the Signal content was a \"public record\" under San Jose (content, not device, controls), and very likely finds the \"no responsive records\" answer violated the CPRA, both because the request itself demanded \"any platform or account\" (giving actual notice that a city-systems-only search was insufficient) and because at least one councilmember concededly still held ~40 responsive screenshots when the answer was given, making \"no responsive records\" false as to existing material. The compound proposition is strong but not certain because CPRA search-duty doctrine for personal-device records remains somewhat unsettled post-San Jose, and a court could still characterize the clerk's initial ignorance of the group's existence as a reasonable search \"at the time,\" blunting (though not eliminating) a violation finding.","rationales":["San Jose holds that the content of a writing, not the device, account, or personal ownership, determines whether it is a \"public record,\" and franchise terms, diversion targets, and RFP timing are core \"conduct of the public's business,\" so the Signal discussions very likely qualify.","The CPRA request expressly sought communications \"on any platform or account,\" which gave the clerk actual notice that a search confined to city servers could not satisfy the Act's good-faith reasonable-search standard.","The \"no responsive records\" answer was demonstrably inaccurate the moment it was given, since one councilmember still possessed roughly 40 screenshots of responsive material, which alone likely establishes a violation independent of the broader public-record/search-methodology questions.","San Jose remanded specifically for development of a reasonable search methodology, and emerging post-remand practice generally requires agencies to inquire of individual custodians once on notice that personal-device records may exist, so a search with zero inquiry to any councilmember falls short of that developing standard.","Countervailing uncertainty remains because a court could find some or all content is preliminary/deliberative and thus exempt from disclosure even though it is a \"public record,\" and could excuse the clerk's initial search as reasonable given no proof the clerk had actual knowledge of the Signal group before this dispute, which would soften a violation finding on the search-adequacy prong even if it does not eliminate it."],"authorities":["Gov. Code §§ 7920.000 et seq. (CPRA, recodified from former § 6250 et seq.) — verify current section numbers","City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608","Gov. Code § 7922.545 / former § 6254(a) (preliminary drafts/notes exemption) — verify recodified citation","ACLU of Northern California v. Superior Court (2011) 202 Cal.App.4th 55 (search adequacy)","Gov. Code § 34090 (records destruction/retention authority for local agencies)","Gov. Code § 7923.100 / former § 6258 (CPRA judicial enforcement — mandate/injunction/declaratory relief)","Gov. Code § 7923.115 / former § 6259 (fee-shifting for prevailing CPRA petitioners) — relevant to remedies question"],"biggest_uncertainty":"Whether California courts will treat \"zero inquiry to individual officials\" as per se unreasonable search under the CPRA absent a settled bright-line duty to canvass personal devices — the precise scope of the post-San Jose search-methodology obligation is still doctrinally underdeveloped, so a court could instead frame this as a forward-looking remedial issue rather than a clear violation at the time the response was given."}

data.position_summary: Starting from the strongest lawful-conduct position — that the clerk reasonably searched "city systems" and had no actual knowledge of a private Signal channel — that defense mostly collapses on these facts. It is very likely a reviewing court holds the Signal content was a "public record" under San Jose (content, not device, controls), and very likely finds the "no responsive records" answer violated the CPRA, both because the request itself demanded "any platform or account" (giving actual notice that a city-systems-only search was insufficient) and because at least one councilmember concededly still held ~40 responsive screenshots when the answer was given, making "no responsive records" false as to existing material. The compound proposition is strong but not certain because CPRA search-duty doctrine for personal-device records remains somewhat unsettled post-San Jose, and a court could still characterize the clerk's initial ignorance of the group's existence as a reasonable search "at the time," blunting (though not eliminating) a violation finding.
rationales: San Jose holds that the content of a writing, not the device, account, or personal ownership, determines whether it is a "public record," and franchise terms, diversion targets, and RFP timing are core "conduct of the public's business," so the Signal discussions very likely qualify.

The CPRA request expressly sought communications "on any platform or account," which gave the clerk actual notice that a search confined to city servers could not satisfy the Act's good-faith reasonable-search standard.

The "no responsive records" answer was demonstrably inaccurate the moment it was given, since one councilmember still possessed roughly 40 screenshots of responsive material, which alone likely establishes a violation independent of the broader public-record/search-methodology questions.

San Jose remanded specifically for development of a reasonable search methodology, and emerging post-remand practice generally requires agencies to inquire of individual custodians once on notice that personal-device records may exist, so a search with zero inquiry to any councilmember falls short of that developing standard.

Countervailing uncertainty remains because a court could find some or all content is preliminary/deliberative and thus exempt from disclosure even though it is a "public record," and could excuse the clerk's initial search as reasonable given no proof the clerk had actual knowledge of the Signal group before this dispute, which would soften a violation finding on the search-adequacy prong even if it does not eliminate it.
authorities: Gov. Code §§ 7920.000 et seq. (CPRA, recodified from former § 6250 et seq.) — verify current section numbers
City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608
Gov. Code § 7922.545 / former § 6254(a) (preliminary drafts/notes exemption) — verify recodified citation
ACLU of Northern California v. Superior Court (2011) 202 Cal.App.4th 55 (search adequacy)
Gov. Code § 34090 (records destruction/retention authority for local agencies)
Gov. Code § 7923.100 / former § 6258 (CPRA judicial enforcement — mandate/injunction/declaratory relief)
Gov. Code § 7923.115 / former § 6259 (fee-shifting for prevailing CPRA petitioners) — relevant to remedies question
data.biggest_uncertainty: Whether California courts will treat "zero inquiry to individual officials" as per se unreasonable search under the CPRA absent a settled bright-line duty to canvass personal devices — the precise scope of the post-San Jose search-methodology obligation is still doctrinally underdeveloped, so a court could instead frame this as a forward-looking remedial issue rather than a clear violation at the time the response was given.
